serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of ginger onion chicken rice typically consists of approximately 200 g of steamed white rice topped with poached or steamed chicken pieces (approximately 150 g) and garnished with ginger-scallion sauce, yielding a total serving weight of approximately 400 g.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken rice (mixed dish) — adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, roasted (NDB 05013) and Rice, white, long-grain, regular, cooked (NDB 20045), combined in proportional ratio with ginger-onion condiment estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Ginger Onion Chicken Rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data: approximately 37.5% cooked chicken (NDB 05013: 165 kcal, 31.0 g protein, 3.6 g fat per 100 g), 50% cooked white rice (NDB 20045: 130 kcal, 2.7 g protein, 0.3 g fat, 28.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g), and 12.5% ginger-onion-soy condiment (estimated 40 kcal, 1.5 g protein, 1.5 g fat, 4.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g). Weighted blend yields the per-100g values below. Confidence: medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White rice, boiled (GI ~54–72, reference glucose); chicken-rice mixed dish estimated at GI 54 based on white rice component dominance moderated by protein and fat co-ingestion
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ginger Onion Chicken Rice. GI estimated at 54 (low) based on the dominant white rice component (GI 54–72 per University of Sydney GI Database for boiled white rice) moderated downward by the substantial protein (chicken) and fat content of the dish, consistent with mixed-meal GI attenuation principles documented in peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 68.0) / 100 = 36.7, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 17.0 × 4 = 68.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 400 g serving provides approximately 1120 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or low-sodium soy sauce and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 36.7 per standard serving is high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion, substituting brown rice, or pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean poultry protein with relatively low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides lean chicken protein with an estimated 0.7 g saturated fat per 100 g, which is consistent with heart-healthy dietary patterns; however, sodium from soy-based condiments should be monitored and portion size controlled to support cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ginger Onion Chicken Rice is a Filipino-style rice dish consisting of steamed or poached chicken served over white rice and dressed with a fragrant ginger-scallion sauce made from finely minced fresh ginger, spring onions (scallions), garlic, soy sauce, and sesame oil. The dish is closely related to the broader Chinese-influenced chicken rice tradition found across Southeast Asia, including Hainanese Chicken Rice in Singapore and Malaysia, but the Filipino version typically features a more pronounced ginger and onion flavour profile and may incorporate local seasoning preferences. The chicken is tender and moist from gentle poaching or steaming, while the rice absorbs the aromatic cooking broth. The ginger-onion condiment provides a sharp, pungent, and slightly savoury contrast to the mild chicken and neutral rice. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, high-protein meal providing approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with lean chicken contributing around 13.3 g protein per 100 g and white rice supplying the primary carbohydrate load of approximately 17.0 g per 100 g. Sodium content is moderate to high due to soy sauce seasoning, making portion control relevant for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic load is moderate to high per full serving owing to the white rice base, which is a consideration for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally dairy-free and typically halal. It is commonly served as a lunch or dinner main course in Filipino cafes and casual dining establishments in Singapore, including Lucky Cafe on North Canal Road.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Ginger Onion Chicken Rice was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values were estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked chicken and cooked white rice in proportional ratios, with a condiment fraction estimated from ingredient knowledge. GI was estimated analogously from the University of Sydney GI Database white rice entry moderated by protein and fat co-ingestion. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data (cooked chicken NDB 05013, cooked white rice NDB 20045) in a weighted blend; no exact composite entry was available in any primary regional source. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this estimation. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this estimation. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this estimation. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in lean poultry and plain rice per USDA SR Legacy data. 6. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated for mixed-meal protein and fat attenuation; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the approved GI database. 7. The food is served at Lucky Cafe, North Canal Road, Singapore, as recorded in the field survey data. 8. The cuisine is classified as Filipino per the input record; the dish shares structural similarities with Hainanese Chicken Rice but is distinct in its ginger-onion flavour emphasis and Filipino cafe context. 9. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.
